Name: quantization_rate_distortion_tradeoff
Title: Exploring the Rate-Distortion Trade-offs of Quantization in Model Compression
Short Hypothesis: Different quantization schemes significantly affect the rate-distortion trade-offs in model compression, influencing the predictive performance and efficiency of deep learning models.
Related Work: While existing works such as 'Population Risk Improvement with Model Compression' and 'Rate-Distortion Theoretic Model Compression' examine model compression and its effects on performance, they do not systematically analyze how various quantization strategies impact the rate-distortion relationship. Our proposal seeks to fill this gap by providing a theoretical and empirical framework for understanding these impacts.
Abstract: This research proposes a comprehensive study of the rate-distortion trade-offs associated with different quantization strategies in the context of model compression for deep learning. By framing the problem as a lossy source coding issue under a distortion constraint, we aim to quantify how different quantization methods influence the efficacy of model compression. We will derive theoretical bounds on the achievable rates for various quantization schemes and investigate their impacts on predictive performance and generalization error. The study will employ both simulated and real-world datasets to validate the proposed theoretical insights and to establish a framework for selecting optimal quantization methods based on their rate-distortion characteristics. Our findings are expected to enrich the understanding of model compression techniques and provide guidelines for designing efficient models that maintain predictive sufficiency.
Experiments: 1. Compare several quantization methods (e.g., uniform vs. non-uniform quantization) against theoretical rate-distortion bounds derived from the information-theoretic framework.
2. Evaluate the predictive performance and generalization error of compressed models using different quantization schemes on benchmark datasets such as CIFAR-10 and ImageNet.
3. Analyze the sensitivity of model performance to quantization noise as a function of the quantization level, measuring distortion through empirical risk.
4. Implement a dynamic quantization strategy that adapts based on the observed input distribution, and assess its performance against static quantization strategies.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. The theoretical bounds may not capture all practical aspects of quantization effects due to model-specific characteristics.
2. Variability in performance across different datasets may complicate generalization of findings.
3. The computational overhead of dynamic quantization strategies may exceed certain practical limits.
